root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

probability spaces

Table of contents

  1. The frequentist interpretation
  2. A first look at the axiomatic interpretation
  3. A crash course in set theory
  4. Probability spaces
  5. The big picture: How do we use probability spaces and distributions?
  6. Discrete and uniform probability measures
  7. Probability histograms
  8. Continuous probability measures
  9. Probability density graphs (univariate versions)
  10. Distribution and quantile functions
  11. Bivariate continuous probability measures
  12. Probability density graphs (bivariate versions)

1. The frequentist interpretation

We often make probabilistic statements in our everyday conversation, such as, “I think there’s a one-in-four chance that it will rain tomorrow,” or, “I have a 99.9% chance of failing the final exam in my class.” Such statements attempt to quantify our subjective belief in some event occurring, but there’s no real mathematical basis for obtaining the specific probability values. After all, could you imagine any realistic method that a person might use to rigorously and objectively obtain the exact probability value of 99.9% mentioned above?!?! Yeah, not likely.

On the other hand, we may try to avoid appeals to subjective opinions and assessments and make probability theory purely mathematical by appealing to long-run relative frequencies. For example, suppose that you roll a fair six-sided die:

What is the probability $P(4)$ that you roll a four? I think most people would answer

\begin{equation}\notag P(4) = \frac{1}{6}. \end{equation}

If somebody asked us to explain our value for $P(4)$, we might say something like:

“Well, since the die is fair, there’s an equal chance of rolling any number. And since there are six possible numbers that we could roll, the probability of rolling any one particular number is one in six.”

This is a somewhat satisfying explanation, but it’s still not entirely justified by mathematics.

Instead, here’s an alternative explanation that’s a little more rigorous: Suppose that we roll the die many times over and over, and that we record the result of each roll. We then compute the following ratio:

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{\text{number of $4$s rolled}}{\text{total number of rolls}}. \end{equation}

This ratio is called the relative frequency of the event that a $4$ is rolled. If the number of rolls is large, what might you expect this ratio to be close to?

To answer this, let’s have a computer do the rolling for us! Here are the results:

Along the horizontal axis you see the values of $n$, where $n$ is the total number of rolls; along the vertical axis, you see the values of the relative frequency of $4$s. For example, when the computer rolled the die $n=20$ times, it appears that the relative frequency is almost exactly $0.2$. But do you notice that as the number of rolls $n$ increases, the relative frequency of $4$s appears to stabilize along the red dashed line? Let’s have the computer go even further, rolling the die up to $n=1000$ times:

Now we can really see that the relative frequency of $4$s is approaching the red dashed line—but this line just happens to be at a value of $1/6 = 0.1\bar{6}$!

This demonstration suggests that if we wanted a semi-rigorous mathematical explanation for the value

\begin{equation}\notag P(4) = \frac{1}{6}, \end{equation}

we might say that the fraction $1/6$ is the limiting value of the relative frequency of $4$s, as the number of rolls $n$ goes off to $\infty$.

This conceptual framework that describes probabilities as long-run relative frequencies has a name: It is called the frequentist interpretation of probability.

This frequentist interpretation—while it has a certain level of pleasing mathematical precision—does not prove to be entirely satisfactory or appropriate in all probabilistic scenarios, however. For example, suppose that we believe there is a one-in-four chance that it will rain tomorrow; in symbols, this statement would read as:

\begin{equation}\notag P(\text{rain}) = \frac{1}{4}. \end{equation}

But how would we interpret the ratio $1/4$ as a long-run relative frequency? We would have to imagine that it is possible to re-live “tomorrow” over and over again, and to count the number of “tomorrows” that it rains. Then, if $P(\text{rain})$ really is $1/4$, according to the frequentist interpretation we should expect that the relative frequency

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{ \text{number of times it rains}}{\text{number of tomorrows}} \end{equation}

to approach $1/4$ as the number of “tomorrows” goes to infinity. But this is clearly absurd! How could we possibly re-live “tomorrow” over and over?!

2. A first look at the axiomatic interpretation

So, as you can see, interpreting exactly what probability means can be a messy affair, with some interpretations and conceptualizations working well in certain scenarios, while failing miserably in others. The way out of this mess is to leave the meaning of probability to the philosophers, and to instead focus on finding rules for handling and manipulating probabilities that we can all agree on. This leads to something called the axiomatic interpretation of probability. While this interpretation is completely rigorous and objective, it can be a bit abstract the first time you see it.

To begin, let’s first agree that all probabilities, no matter what they really mean, should be represented by numbers between $0$ and $1$. So, our first axiom is:

Probability Axiom 1. Probabilities are represented by real numbers between $0$ and $1$, inclusive.

It is also natural to assume that the probability that some outcome occurs (something happens!) should be $1$. Let’s turn this into an axiom:

Probability Axiom 2. The probability that some outcome occurs is $1$.

It turns out that we only need one more axiom to develop our abstract theory of probability. To make this axiom seem plausible, let’s return to our die-rolling scenario described above.

I think all of us would agree that the probability of rolling an odd number is $1/2$, since precisely half of the numbers are odd. In symbols, we would write:

\begin{equation}\notag P(\text{odd}) = \frac{1}{2}. \end{equation}

A frequentist would confirm this by rolling the die many times, and tracking the long-run relative frequency:

Sure enough, it appears that the relative frequency of rolling an odd is approaching the value $1/2 = 0.5$.

But the question I want to consider now is: What is the probability of rolling a $4$ or an odd? In symbols:

\begin{equation}\notag P(\text{$4$ or odd}) =\ ? \end{equation}

To answer this question, let’s have a look at three different long-run relative frequencies:

You can see that the relative frequency of 4s approaches $1/6=0.1\bar{6}$, as we already knew, and also the relative frequency of odds approaches $1/2=0.5$. But notice that the relative frequency of the event “4 or odd” appears to approach a number between 0.6 and 0.7, perhaps a bit closer to 0.7 than 0.6.

But wait a minute! We know

\begin{equation}\notag P(4) = \frac{1}{6} \quad \text{and} \quad P(\text{odd}) = \frac{1}{2}, \end{equation}

right? Notice that

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{1}{6}+ \frac{1}{2} = \frac{2}{3} = 0.\bar{6}, \end{equation}

so it appears that we have

\begin{equation}\notag P(\text{$4$ or odd}) = P(4) + P(\text{odd}). \end{equation}

This equation suggests that the probability of one event happening, or another, is simply the sum of the two individual probabilities. But, crucially, the two events cannot have any outcomes in common, for otherwise this rule does not work. For example, the probability of rolling a 4 or an even number is just $1/2$, because the event of rolling a $4$ is included in the event of rolling an even.

Now, I hope all of this makes the third and final axiom seem quite natural:

Probability Axiom 3. The probability of one or the other of two disjoint events occurring is the sum of the individual probabilities.

Here, the word disjoint simply means that there is no single outcome that is included in both events. Think of the examples above, of rolling a 4, rolling an odd, and rolling an even. The two events

\begin{equation}\notag \{\text{roll a $4$}\}, \quad \{\text{roll an odd}\}, \end{equation}

are disjoint, while the events

\begin{equation}\notag \{\text{roll a $4$}\}, \quad \{\text{roll an even}\}, \end{equation}

are not disjoint, because the first event is entirely contained in the second.

And there we have it! As I will begin to show you in the rest of this chapter, these three axioms of probability may be used to develop a very sophisticated and powerful theory. And these axioms do not suffer from the same sort of interpretation of meaning that, for example, the frequentist interpretation suffers from. This is because, in the axiomatic interpretation, probability has no inherent meaning. It is only when the abstract theory is applied to real-world scenarios that the probabilities obtain meaning, but the mathematical theory itself does not rely in any way on meaning or interpretation.

3. A crash course in set theory

We’ve collected our three axioms that together constitute the axiomatic interpretation of probability. My goal in the rest of this chapter is to formalize them using the language and objects of set theory. So, I ask for your patience for a moment while review the basics of set theory.

First:

Definition. A set is a set.

Yes, you read that right. This is a laughably circular definition, but my point is simply that a set—for our purposes, at our level—is just a set of objects as you would understand in everyday life. There’s nothing deep here; don’t overthink it.

Now, let’s go through the fundamental operations and definitions in set theory:

  • Set notation. Sets are usually represented with capital letters, and the members of a set are enclosed in curly braces. For example, \begin{equation}\notag A = \{1, 2, 3\} \end{equation} is the set containing the numbers $1$, $2$, and $3$, while \begin{equation}\notag B = \{ \text{June}, \text{July}, \text{August}\} \end{equation} is the set containing the three summer months. The things inside a set are called elements or points of the set, and the symbol “$\in$” is used to indicate that something is in a set. For example, I would write \begin{equation}\notag 1 \in A \quad \text{and} \quad \text{June} \in B \end{equation} to indicate that the number $1$ is in the set $A$ and the month June is in the set $B$, while I would write \begin{equation}\notag 4 \notin A \quad \text{and} \quad \text{March} \notin B \end{equation} to indicate that $A$ does not contain $4$ and that $B$ does not contain March.

  • Set-builder notation. A convenient way to define the elements of a set is via something called set-builder notation. Here’s an example: \begin{equation}\notag A = \{ x\in \bbr : x\geq 0\}. \end{equation} The colon “$:$” is pronounced “such that.” So, in plain English, this set contains all real numbers $x\in \bbr$ such that $x\geq 0$. Thus, the set $A$ is nothing but the half-closed interval $[0,\infty)$. Set-builder notation is usually always written in this format: \begin{equation}\notag \{ \text{type of element} : \text{condition the element must satisfy}\}. \end{equation} For another example, consider the following set (remember that $\bbz$ denotes the set of all integers): \begin{equation}\notag B = \{ n\in \bbz : n = 2k \text{ for some $k\in \bbz$}\}. \end{equation} In plain English, $B$ is the set of all integers $x\in \bbz$ such that $n = 2k$ for some integer $k\in \bbz$. But if $n$ is an integer multiple of $2$, it must be even. So $B$ is just the set of all even integers!

  • Subsets. Sometimes every element of a set $A$ might be an element in a bigger set $S$. When this happens, we write \begin{equation}\notag A \subset S \end{equation} and say that $A$ is a subset of the set $S$. For example, we have \begin{equation}\notag \{1,2,3\} \subset \{1,2,3,4\}, \end{equation} while \begin{equation}\notag \{1,2,3\} \not\subset \{1,2\}, \end{equation} where the diagonal slash through the subset symbol $\subset$ means “not a subset.” If we picture sets as blobs in Venn diagrams, then to say that $A$ is a subset of $S$ means that:

However, if $A$ is not a subset of $S$, then we would draw something like:

  • Unions. If $A$ and $B$ are two sets, the set of elements that are in $A$ or $B$ is called the union of $A$ and $B$ and is denoted $A\cup B$. In terms of a Venn diagram, we have:

For a specific example, suppose \begin{equation}\notag A = \{1,2,3\} \quad \text{and} \quad B = \{ 3, \text{June}, \text{July}\}. \end{equation} Then \begin{equation}\notag A \cup B = \{ 1,2,3, \text{June}, \text{July}\}. \end{equation} Notice that even though $3$ appears as an element in both $A$ and $B$, it only appears once in the union $A\cup B$. This is because sets ignore repetition.

  • Intersections. If $A$ and $B$ are two sets, the set of elements that are in $A$ and $B$ is called the intersection of $A$ and $B$ and is denoted $A\cap B$. In terms of a Venn diagram, we have:

With the example sets \begin{equation}\notag A = \{1,2,3\} \quad \text{and} \quad B = \{ 3, \text{June}, \text{July}\}. \end{equation} from above, we would have \begin{equation}\notag A \cap B = \{ 3\} \end{equation} since $3$ is the only element that appears in both $A$ and $B$.

  • The empty set. We write $\emptyset$ to denote the empty set, the set with no elements. To see how this set is used, note that if \begin{equation}\notag A = \{1,2,3\} \quad \text{and} \quad B = \{ \text{June}, \text{July}, \text{August}\}, \end{equation} then \begin{equation}\notag A \cap B = \emptyset. \end{equation} This equation simply says that the set of elements that are in $A$ and $B$ is empty, since there’s no one element that is in both!

This leads us to make a definition of a term that we saw in the previous section:

Definition. Two sets $A$ and $B$ are called disjoint if their intersection is empty, i.e., \begin{equation}\notag A \cap B = \emptyset. \end{equation}

Continuing with our review of set theory:

  • Complements. Suppose that $A$ is a subset of a set $S$. Then the complement of $A$ in $S$ is the set of elements that are in $S$, but not in $A$. It is denoted $S\smallsetminus A$. In terms of a Venn diagram:

For a specific example, suppose that \begin{equation}\notag A = \{1,2,3 \} \quad \text{and} \quad S = \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}. \end{equation} Then \begin{equation}\notag S\smallsetminus A = \{ 4, 5, 6\}. \end{equation} In order to shorten notation, we will often write $A^c$ to stand for the complement $S\smallsetminus A$, where the superscript “$c$” stands for “complement.”

  • Functions. A function is a “machine” $f$ that takes elements of one set $D$ (called the domain) as inputs, and returns outputs contained in another set $C$ (called the codomain). This is represented symbolically by writing \begin{equation}\notag f:D \to C. \end{equation} Notice that this notation only specifies the name of the function and its domain and codomain, but it does not specify exactly how the function manufactures outputs from inputs. For example, if I write \begin{equation}\notag f:\bbr \to \bbr, \end{equation} then all you know is that the function $f$ takes real numbers as inputs and spits out real numbers as outputs. In order to nail down the function precisely, I would need to describe how the function accomplishes this task. For example, I could write $f(x) = x^2$, which completely specifies the function.

  • Cardinality. The cardinality of a set $A$ is simply the number of elements that it contains, and is represented by absolute value bars $|A|$. For example, if \begin{equation}\notag A = \{a, b,c\}, \end{equation} then the cardinality of $A$ is $|A|=3$. Of course, some sets contain infinitely many elements, in which case the cardinality of the set is $\infty$.

That’s it for the definitions! We’ll get some further practice with these concepts as we work through the worksheets in class.

Problem Prompt. Now, I would suggest that you tackle problems 1 and 2 in worksheet.

4. Probability spaces

With the notation and terminology of set theory in hand, we are now ready to assemble and package our three axioms of probability into a new mathematical object called a probability space.

But before we launch into the abstract definition, it will be worth reviewing what we’re trying to accomplish. Basically, everything in this chapter revolves around the following question:

Q: What is probability?

In the first section of this chapter, we saw the frequentist interpretation of probability, which, with the goal of making probability seem less subjective, conceives of probabilities as long-run relative frequencies. But there are problems with this interpretation, because to compute the long-run relative frequency of an event, you must imagine the event occurring over and over. But clearly this doesn’t make sense in all situations and scenarios!

This suggests that the answer to the fundamental question “What is probability?” might be more difficult to come by than you might have first imagined. So, instead of getting lost in the philosophical weeds struggling to cook up a good answer, we essentially side-step the question:

Q: What is probability?

A: I don’t know. But I do know that probabilities, no matter what they are, should follow a simple set of three rules, or axioms:

  1. Probabilities are represented by real numbers between $0$ and $1$, inclusive.

  2. The probability that some outcome occurs is $1$.

  3. The probability of one or the other of two disjoint events occurring is the sum of the individual probabilities.

So, our goal in this section is to take these three axioms and turn them into precise mathematics written in the language of set theory.

To cut straight to the chase, the three axioms will be distilled and encoded in a mathematical gadget called a probability measure, which makes up one part of the following type of object:

Definition. A probability space consists of three things:

  1. A set $S$ called the sample space.

  2. A collection of subsets of $S$, called events.

  3. A probability measure $P$.

Of course, we don’t yet know what any of these words mean!

  • What is a sample space?!

  • What is an event?!?

  • What is a probability measure!?!!?!?!

But don’t worry! Over the rest of this section, I will walk you through each of these three pieces and explain them in detail.

Let’s begin with a sample space $S$. You should imagine that $S$ is the set of all outcomes of some process. We will go through several examples in:

Problem Prompt. Do problem 3 on the worksheet.

Even though all the sample spaces that we listed in our solution to this problem correspond to the outcomes of some real-world process, scenario, or experiment, an abstract sample space $S$ just has to be a set. It doesn’t actually have to correspond to anything real. Remember, we are working in the abstract, mathematical realm!

Let’s now move on to the second piece of a probability space: Events.

Definition. Let $S$ be a sample space.

  • The elements of $S$ are called sample points.

  • Subsets of $S$ are called events.

  • An event containing just one sample point is called a simple event. All other events are called compound events.

If we conceptualize a sample space $S$ as consisting of all possible outcomes of some process, then a sample point in $S$ is the same thing as an outcome. So, a simple event consists of just one possible outcome, while compound events consist of multiple outcomes.

Looking beyond… Technically, this definition of an event is almost correct. There’s actually something that needs to be added in the case of a “non-discrete” probability space that restricts which subsets are allowed to be events. But we don’t worry about that stuff at this level.

Let’s go through some examples of events in:

Problem Prompt. Do problems 4-6 on the worksheet.

Before we move on to talk about the third ingredient in a probability space, let me add one more important thing about events:

Properties of Events. Let $S$ be a sample space.

  1. If $A$ is an event, then so too is its complement $S\smallsetminus A$.

  2. The entire sample space itself is always an event, and so is the empty set $\emptyset$.

  3. If $A$ and $B$ are events, then so too is the union $A\cup B$ and intersection $A\cap B$.

  4. In fact, if $A_1,A_2,A_3,\ldots$ is an infinite sequence of events, then the infinite union \begin{equation}\notag A_1 \cup A_2 \cup A_3 \cup \cdots \end{equation} and the infinite intersection \begin{equation}\notag A_1 \cap A_2 \cap A_3 \cap \cdots \end{equation} are also events.

These properties are essentially here for logical consistency and won’t play a giant role in our course. In deeper treatments of probability theory they become much more important, and they are connected with the technicality I mentioned above regarding what subsets are allowed to be events in a “non-discrete” probability space. (If you want a glimpse of what I’m talking about, look here, but don’t expect it to make any sense.)

Onward to the third (and final!) ingredient of a probability space!

Definition. Let $S$ be a sample space. A probability measure $P$ (also called a probability distribution) is a function that to each event $A$ in $S$ assigns a number $P(A)$, called the probability of $A$, subject to the following axioms:

  1. $P(A) \geq 0$ for all events $A$.

  2. $P(S)=1$.

  3. If $A_1,A_2,A_3,\ldots$ is a sequence of pairwise disjoint events in $S$, then \begin{equation}\notag P(A_1 \cup A_2 \cup A_3 \cup \cdots) = \sum_{i=1}^\infty P(A_i). \end{equation}

Now, even though it might not be clear at first glance, these defining axioms of a probability measure are actually the embryonic set-theoretic forms of our three probability axioms! It takes a bit of work to coax our axioms out of them, but trust me, they’re there.

In fact, not only do our three probability axioms flow from them, but a few other nice properties of probability too:

Properties of Probability Measures. Let $P$ be a probability measure on a sample space $S$.

  1. If $A$ is an event, then $P(A)\leq 1$.

  2. If $A$ and $B$ are events and $A\subset B$, then $P(A) \leq P(B)$.

  3. If $A$ is an event, then $P(A^c) = 1-P(A)$.

  4. If $A$ and $B$ are disjoint events, then $P(A\cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$.

  5. We have $P(\emptyset)=0$.

It takes a good amount of work to prove that these properties all follow from the three defining axioms of a probability measure. The proofs are contained in your textbook, if you’d like to have a look. (Better yet, prove them on your own.)

Notice, in particular, that the first property above combined with the first axiom of a probability measure together show that all probabilities are between $0$ and $1$, which was our first axiom of probabilities. The second axiom of a probability measure (the one that states $P(S)=1$) says precisely that the probability that some outcome occurs is $1$, because $S$ consists of all outcomes. Finally, the third axiom for probability is exactly the fourth property listed in the box above.

Ok. Before moving on, let’s recap.

The first thing to make sure you understand is that a probability measure $P$ is nothing but a function which takes events as inputs and spits out probabilities:

Using our notation for functions, I might even write this as:

\begin{equation}\notag P: \{\text{all events}\} \to [0,1]. \end{equation}

Notice the domain of $P$ is the set of all events, while the codomain is the closed interval of all real numbers between $0$ and $1$.

You also need to remember that a probability space is actually a package that consists of three things:

  1. A sample space $S$.

  2. A collection of events.

  3. A probability measure $P$.

If you’re missing even one of these ingredients, then you do not have a probability space.

Warning! Having said this, I will often refer to the sample space $S$ as if it were the probability space, even though this is technically incorrect. This will help shorten the language and cut down on verbosity, and it should not cause any great confusion.

Finally, let me note that it is possible for the same set $S$ to serve as the sample spaces for two different probability spaces if the probability measures are different. We will see this occur below when we talk about continuous probability measures.

5. The big picture: How do we use probability spaces and distributions?

As we go through this class, you should imagine that you are filling up your toolkit with various special types of probability spaces and distributions. Then, when you’re confronted with some real-world scenario that you’d like to model, you reach into your toolkit and find the correct probability distribution which fits the scenario. The challenge, of course, is to figure out exactly which one works the best!

This is really hard, so do not despair if it’s difficult at first! Professional statisticians and applied mathematicians spend a huge part of their professional lives struggling with this question!

6. Discrete and uniform probability measures

So, let’s begin filling up our probabilistic toolkit!

The first types of probability measures that we shall study are the discrete ones. To define them, first we need a definition:

Definition. Let $S$ be any set and $p:S\to \bbr$ a function.

  1. The support of $p$ is the set of all points $s\in S$ where $p(s)$ is nonzero, i.e., it is the set \begin{equation}\label{supp-eqn} \{s\in S : p(s) \neq 0\}. \end{equation}

  2. The function $p$ is said to have discrete support if its support \eqref{supp-eqn} is either finite or countably infinite.

But wait a minute! What does “countably infinite” mean?

Definiton (sort of). A set $A$ is countably infinite if, given an infinite amount of time, I could count the elements of $A$ one at a time, counting one element per second.

Of course, this isn’t really a precise mathematical definition, but I think it gets the idea across. It just means that the set $A$ is countably infinite if its elements can be paired off one-by-one with the set of positive whole numbers

\begin{equation}\notag \{1,2,3,\ldots\}. \end{equation}

For example, I could count an infinite sequence of bananas, so this sequence would be considered countably infinite:

On the other hand, any interval of real numbers, including the entire real line $\bbr$ itself, is uncountably infinite. For example, consider the interval $[0,1]$:

Indeed, if we are counting the numbers in $[0,1]$ from left to right, we would be tempted to choose $0$ as the first number—but what would the second number be? And, even if you managed to think of a good candidate for the second number, you’d never be able to count all numbers in $[0,1]$, one at a time, one per second, even if you had an infinite amount of time. There are actually rigorous mathematical proofs of this!

We are now ready to define discrete probability measures:

Definition. Let $P$ be a probability measure on a sample space $S$. We shall say $P$ is discrete if every subset $A\subset S$ is an event and there is a function $p:S\to \bbr$ with discrete support such that

\begin{equation}\label{disc-eqn} P(A) = \sum_{s\in A} p(s), \end{equation}

for all events $A$. In this case, the function $p$ is called the probability function of the probability measure $P$, and $S$ is called a discrete probability space (when equipped with $P$).

Intuitively, a probability measure is discrete if the probability is “concentrated” on a discrete set of sample points, and isn’t smeared out continuously over the entire probability space. (The words discrete and continuous are often treated as opposites in mathematics.) The probability function $p(s)$ is often also called the probability mass function (PMF), although our textbook doesn’t appear to like this terminology.

The formula \eqref{disc-eqn} might initially look confusing, but it’s actually quite simple. It just means this:

  • If you want to compute the probability $P(A)$ of an event $A$, simply find all sample points $s\in A$, compute $p(s)$ for each of these $s\in A$, and then add them all up.

If the support of $p$ is finite, then you will only be adding finitely many values $p(s)$. On the other hand, if the support of $p$ is countably infinite, then the sum \eqref{disc-eqn} turns into an infinite series! We will see examples of this in:

Problem Prompt. Do problems 7 and 8 on the worksheet.

Now, I want to state some properties of probability functions:

Properties of Probability Functions. Let $p(s)$ be the probability function of a discrete probability measure $P$. Then:

  1. $p(s) \geq 0$ for all $s\in S$, and

  2. $\sum_{s\in S} p(s) =1$.

To see why (1) is true, suppose it were not true, and that there exists a sample point $s\in S$ with $p(s)<0$. Then, the simple event $\{s\}$ would have negative probability:

\begin{equation}\notag P(\{s\}) = p(s) <0. \end{equation}

But this cannot happen, and thus we must have $p(s) \geq 0$ for all $s\in S$. To see why (2) is true, observe that it follows immediately from \eqref{disc-eqn} and the fact $P(S)=1$.

You might be concerned that I did not mention explicitly that $p(s) \leq 1$ (for all $s\in S$). But this inequality is actually a consequence of (1) and (2). Do you see why?

The two properties of probability functions identified above are very important, because they essentially characterize all functions that can serve as probability functions for some discrete probability measure. This is the content of the following important result that we will use over and over:

Discrete Probability Construction Lemma. Let $S$ be a set and $p:S \to \bbr$ a function with discrete support. If

  1. $p(s) \geq 0$ for all $s\in S$, and

  2. $\sum_{s\in S} p(s) =1$,

then there is a unique discrete probability measure $P$ on $S$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag P(A) = \sum_{s\in A} p(s) \end{equation}

for all $A\subset S$.

The gist of this Construction Lemma is that, in order to construct a discrete probability measure on a set $S$, all we need to do is define an appropriate probability function.

To explain further, for simplicity let’s consider the case of a finite set $S$ consisting of just four points:

\begin{equation}\notag S = \{s_1,s_2,s_3,s_4\}. \end{equation}

Technically, according to the definition, if we wanted to define a (discrete) probability measure $P$ on $S$, we would need to specify the probability $P(A)$ for all subsets $A\subset S$. But there are $2^4 = 16$ such subsets! Instead, according to the Discrete Probability Construction Lemma, in order to define $P$ uniquely, all we need to do is define the probability function $p(s)$ by specifying the four probabilities

\begin{equation}\notag p(s_1), p(s_2), p(s_3), p(s_4) \geq 0, \end{equation}

while making sure that they sum to $1$. For example, we could do it this way:

Not so bad, right? The Discrete Probability Construction Lemma might look intimidating, but it’s really quite simple. And it’s precisely this simplicity afforded by the Construction Lemma that makes discrete probability spaces so easy to work with, compared to more complicated probability spaces that we will encounter later.

There are even nicer versions of discrete probability measures!

Definition. Let $P$ be a discrete probability measure on a sample space $S$ with probability function $p(s)$. Then $P$ is called a uniform probability measure if the support of $p(s)$ has finite cardinality $n>0$, and if

\begin{equation}\notag p(s) = \frac{1}{n} \end{equation}

for each $s$ in the support of $p$.

Continuing with our four-element set $S$ above, a uniform probability measure simply gives identical probabilities to each point in the support of the probability function. If we suppose that each $s_k$ ($k=1,2,3,4$) is in the support of $p$, then each $s_k$ would receive probability $1/4$:

Problem Prompt. Do problems 9-11 on the worksheet.

7. Probability histograms

In the previous section, I drew little cartoon pictures to help us conceptualize discrete probability measures and spaces. But, at least when the sample space is the real line $S=\bbr$, it turns out that there is a much more useful way to visualize discrete probability measures using probability histograms.

To illustrate, let’s take a simple example from the worksheet. On the real line $S=\bbr$, recall that we had a discrete probability measure $P$ with probability function

\begin{equation}\notag p(s) = \begin{cases} (0.25)(0.75)^{s-1} & : s=1,2,\ldots, \\ 0 & : \text{otherwise}.\end{cases} \end{equation}

In the real world, this abstract probability space models the probability of the number of flips of a coin it would take before you obtain heads, assuming that the probability of obtaining heads is $0.25$. (By the way, this is an example of something called a geometric distribution with parameter $p=0.25$, which we will discuss in more detail later.)

Since the sample space is $\bbr$, we can imagine it as the horizontal axis in the coordinate plane $\bbr^2$. Then, above each $s$ in the support of $p(s)$, we draw a little vertical rectangle whose height is the probability $p(s)$. For example:

So, since $p(1)=0.25$, you see that there is a rectangle of height $0.25$ above $s=1$. Notice that there are no rectangles above $s=0$ or any negative integer $s$; or, more accurately, the rectangles above these integers have height $=0$. Make sure also to notice that none of the rectangles have heights bigger than $1$!

What does a uniform probability measure on $\bbr$ look like? Very simple:

This is a histogram of the uniform probability measure on $\bbr$ with probability function $p(s)$ with

\begin{equation}\notag \text{support} = \{1,2,3,4,5,6\} \quad \text{and} \quad p(s) = \frac{1}{6}. \end{equation}

These probability histograms can really only be drawn when the sample space is $S=\bbr$. Obviously, if the sample space is something more exotic, like $\bbr^{35}$, probability histograms don’t make sense.

8. Continuous probability measures

Discrete probability measures are the first important type of probability measure that we will study. But there is another type, the so-called continuous probability measures, that will also be of great interest to us.

To help motivate their definition, let’s suppose that we have a discrete probability measure $P$ on the real line $S = \bbr$. Since $P$ is discrete, the intuition is that all the probability is “concentrated” on a discrete set of points along $\bbr$:

Imagine now that we want to take the probability on these discrete points and “smear it out” over the entire real line:

How might we do this?

Recall that the probability function $p(s)$ of a discrete probability measure must sum to $1$:

\begin{equation}\label{sum-eqn} \sum_{s\in \bbr} p(s) =1. \end{equation}

But if we attempt to “smear” the probability off the discrete support of $p(s)$ and onto a continuum, however, we run into the following mathematical fact:

If the support of a function $p:\bbr \to [0,\infty)$ is NOT discrete, then it is IMPOSSIBLE for the equation \eqref{sum-eqn} to hold.

This amazing fact is due to the fundamental difference between countably infinite sets and uncountably infinite sets. Essentially, if the support of $p(s)$ is uncountably infinite, then there are so many numbers in the sum \eqref{sum-eqn} that it is impossible for them to add up to $1$ (or any other finite number), no matter how small they all are.

This fact puts us in a real pickle if we want to “smear out” the probability onto the entire real line $\bbr$. It says, basically, that we have to give up on the possibility of having a meaningful probability function $p(s)$!

It turns out the way forward is to also give up on talking about probability at individual points of $\bbr$ (these probabilities will all end up being $0$), and to only talk about probabilities over intervals. Accordingly, a probability density function, denoted $f(s)$, replaces the doomed probability function $p(s)$. Even though everything is unitless, you should imagine that $f(s)$ really is an honest density function:

\begin{equation}\notag \text{units of $f(s)$} = \frac{\text{probability}}{\text{length}}. \end{equation}

Then, the probability of an interval $[a,b]$ should be nothing but the length of the interval, $b-a$, times the density function. But there’s a problem with this simple description, since the value of the density function $f(s)$ might vary as $s$ passes over $[a,b]$. To account for this variability, we use one of the lessons that we’ve internalized from calculus and compute probabilities by integration against the density:

\begin{equation}\notag P\big( [a,b] \big) = \int_a^b f(s) \ \d s. \end{equation}

Notice, in particular, that

\begin{equation}\notag P(\{a\}) = P\big([a,a] \big) = \int_a^a f(s) \ \d s = 0, \end{equation}

which shows—as I mentioned above—that the probability of an individual sample point is $0$.

This discussion leads us to:

Definition. Let $P$ be a probability measure on $\bbr$. We shall say $P$ is continuous if there is a function $f:\bbr \to \bbr$ such that

\begin{equation}\label{cont-eqn} P\big( [a,b] \big) = \int_a^b f(s) \ \d s \end{equation}

for all $-\infty \leq a \leq b \leq +\infty$. In this case, the function $f(s)$ is called the probability density function (PDF) of the probability measure $P$, and $\bbr$ is called a continuous probability space (when equipped with $P$).

Before we get to examples, I must make several very important remarks and observations. Please take time to carefully read and understand them all!

If you review the definition of a discrete probability measure, then you’ll notice that this new definition is very similar in spirit: Indeed, whereas a discrete probability measure is defined in terms of the existence of a probability function $p(s)$, a continuous probability measure is defined in terms of the existence of a probability density function $f(s)$. Really, the big differences between the two definitions are:

  • Continuous probability measures can only live on $\bbr$.

  • The sum $\sum_{s\in A}$ in a discrete probability measure is replaced with an integral $\int_a^b$ in a continuous probability measure.

Notice that we allow $a = -\infty$ and $b = +\infty$ in the definition, so that equation \eqref{cont-eqn} would yield \begin{equation}\notag P\big( (-\infty,b) \big) = \int_{-\infty}^b f(y) \ \d y \quad \text{and} \quad P\big( (a,+\infty) \big) = \int_{a}^{+\infty} f(y) \ \d y, \end{equation} and \begin{equation}\notag P(\bbr) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(y) \ \d y, \end{equation} where the integrals are all improper integrals.

In contrast to discrete probability measures, notice that I did not mention which subsets of $\bbr$ are allowed to be events. This would lead to a very technical discussion that is not at all enlightening at this stage. All you need to know is that any “reasonable” subset $A$ of $\bbr$ is allowed to be an event.

However, you might protest that the formula \eqref{cont-eqn} only works for events that are intervals—how would we use this formula to compute probabilities of events that are not intervals? The answer is: Essentially all the events that we will consider in this class will be intervals, or simple unions of intervals. In the general case, to compute $P(A)$ where $A$ is any type of event, we would need to factor an indicator function into the integral. But we don’t talk about that stuff at this level. It won’t be important.

In order for the integral \eqref{cont-eqn} to even exist, we need to assume that the density function $f(s)$ is integrable (and have a few other properties, listed below). However, the density function $f(s)$ itself does not need to be continuous. When we say that a probability measure $P$ is continuous, we are referring to $P$ being continuous, not its density function $f(s)$.

Remember that a probability space consists of a sample space and a choice of probability measure. Thus, the real line $\bbr$ can be a continuous probability space in multiple ways, simply by equipping it with different probability measures.

Finally, let me note that the probability function $p(s)$ of a discrete probability measure and the probability density function $f(s)$ of a continuous probability measure are very similar in some ways, but yet very different in others. For example:

  • Discrete probability measures are computed by summing “against” the probability function $p(s)$. Likewise, continuous probability measures are computed by integrating “against” the probability density function $f(s)$.

  • But as I mentioned at the beginning of this section, whereas the values of $p(s)$ are actual probabilities, the values of $f(s)$ are not probabilities. For example, if I stick $s=2$ into the probability density function and obtain the number $0.5= f(2)$, this $0.5$ is not(!) the probability of the simple event $\{2\}$. (In fact, we saw above that the probability of any simple event in a continuous probability space is $0$.) Instead, the value $0.5$ is the probability density at $s=2$.

In fact, it is possible for probability density functions to take on values bigger than $1$, which are illegal values for probabilities.

Problem Prompt. Do problems 12 and 13 on the worksheet.

It turns out that probability density functions have similar properties compared to probability functions:

Properties of Probability Density Functions (univariate version). Let $f(s)$ be the probability density function of a continuous probability measure $P$ on $\bbr$. Then:

  1. $f(s) \geq 0 $ for all $s\in \bbr$, and

  2. $\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(s) \ \d s =1$.

Conversely, any function $f$ with these two properties defines a unique continuous probability measure:

Continuous Probability Construction Lemma (univariate version). Let $f:\bbr \to \bbr$ be a function such that

  1. $f(s)\geq 0$ for all $s\in \bbr$, and

  2. $\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(s) \ \d s =1$.

Then there is a unique continuous probability measure $P$ on $\bbr$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag P\big( [a,b] \big) = \int_a^b f(s) \ \d s \end{equation}

for $-\infty \leq a \leq b \leq +\infty$.

We will not prove either of these previous two results. They should at least be plausible, however.

Problem Prompt. Do problem 14 on the worksheet.

9. Probability density graphs (univariate versions)

When we discussed discrete probability measures on the sample space $\bbr$, we saw that probability histograms provide convenient ways to visualize probability. For continuous probability measures, however, probability histograms no longer make sense (because the probability of every simple event is $0$). Instead, we use probability density graphs.

As an example, let’s take the continuous probability measure on $\bbr$ with density

\begin{equation}\notag f(s) = \begin{cases} \frac{2}{9}s & : 0\leq s \leq 3, \\ 0 & : \text{otherwise.}\end{cases} \end{equation}

This was one of the densities that we studied in the worksheet problems. The associated probability density graph is then nothing but the graph of $f(s)$:

Because $f(s)$ outputs probability densities, and not probabilities themselves, the probability of an event $A\subset \bbr$ is the area under the density curve and above $A$. For example, the shaded area in the graph above is the following probability:

\begin{equation}\notag P\big((1,2.75) \big) = \int_1^{2.75} f(s) \ \d s \approx 0.73. \end{equation}

It is very important to remember:

The heights of the density curve are not probabilities.

I recommend repeating this over and over in your head, like a mantra.

As another example, consider the density:

\begin{equation}\notag f(s) = \begin{cases} \displaystyle\frac{1}{s^2} & : s\geq 1, \\ 0 & : \text{otherwise.}\end{cases} \end{equation}

This density is also from the worksheet problems. The probability density curve is:

The shaded area represents the following probability:

\begin{equation}\notag P\big( [2,4] \big) = \int_2^4 f(s) \ \d s = \frac{1}{4}. \end{equation}

10. Distribution and quantile functions

We have seen that discrete probability measures have their probability functions, while continuous probability measures (on $\bbr$) have their density functions. In this section, we study a type of function called a distribution function that may be associated with any probability measure on $\bbr$, whether the measure is discrete, continuous, or neither.

Definition. A distribution function of a probability measure $P$ on $\bbr$ is a function $F:\bbr \to \bbr$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag F(s) = P \big( (-\infty,s]\big). \end{equation}

In particular:

  1. If $P$ is discrete with probability function $p(s)$, then \begin{equation}\label{yuy-eqn} F(s) = \sum_{t \leq s} p(t), \end{equation} where the sum ranges over all $t\in \bbr$ with $t \leq s$.

  2. If $P$ is continuous with probability density function $f(s)$, then \begin{equation}\notag F(s) = \int_{-\infty}^s f(t) \ \d t. \end{equation}

Distribution functions are also frequently called cumulative distribution functions (CDFs).

Luckily, since distribution functions are real-valued functions on $\bbr$, we can draw their graphs to help understand them. Let’s start with a simple example of the discrete probability measure $P$ on described by the following probability histogram:

Now, if $s$ is a number that is less than $2$, then $F(s)=0$, simply because there are no $t\in \bbr$ less than $2$ with $p(t)\neq 0$; thus, from \eqref{yuy-eqn}, we must have $F(s)=0$. However, we do have

\begin{equation}\notag F(2) = \sum_{-\infty < t \leq 2} p(t) = p(2) = 0.1. \end{equation}

Likewise, we have $F(s) = 0.1$ for all $s$ with $2\leq s <3$, because there are no $t\in \bbr$ with $2<t<3$ and $p(t)\neq 0$. But, as soon as $s=3$, we pick up the term $p(3) = 0.2$ in the sum \eqref{yuy-eqn}, and we get

\begin{equation}\notag F(3) = \sum_{-\infty < t \leq 3} p(t) = p(2) + p(3) = 0.3. \end{equation}

Do you see how this game is played? If you continue on with this line of reasoning, you will eventually cook up the following expression for the distribution function:

\begin{equation}\notag F(s) = \begin{cases} 0 & : s < 2, \\ 0.1 & : 2 \leq s < 3, \\ 0.3 & : 3\leq s < 4, \\ 0.6 & : 4 \leq s < 5, \\ 1 & : 5 \leq s. \end{cases} \end{equation}

If we graph $F(s)$, we get a ‘step function’:

Do you see now why $F(s)$ is sometimes called a cumulative distribution function? According to the dictionary definition, the word ‘cumulative’ means ‘increasing or increased in quantity, degree, or force by successive additions.’ It is the last part—successive additions—which is the reason why $F(s)$ is called a cumulative distribution function.

Step functions are characteristic of the distribution functions of discrete probability measures—I will state this formally below. On the other hand, suppose now we turn toward a continuous probability measure $P$, like the one we studied in the previous section with density

\begin{equation}\notag f(s) = \begin{cases} \displaystyle \frac{1}{s^2} & : s\geq 1, \\ 0 & : \text{otherwise}.\end{cases} \end{equation}

For a reminder, here is the graph of $f(s)$:

Then, by definition, we have

\begin{equation}\notag F(s) = \int_{-\infty}^s f(t) \ \d t = \int_1^s \frac{1}{t^2} \ \d t = - \frac{1}{s} + 1 \end{equation}

provided that $s\geq 1$, while $F(s)=0$ if $s<1$. Thus,

\begin{equation}\notag F(s) = \begin{cases} -\displaystyle\frac{1}{s} + 1 & : s\geq 1, \\ 0 & : s <1 .\end{cases} \end{equation}

If we graph $F(s)$, we get:

In contrast to the distribution function above, notice that this one is continuous. So, we have noted that the discrete probability measure above has a distribution function which is a step function, while the continuous probability measure above has a distribution function which is itself continuous. These are not coincidences! In fact:

Properties of Distribution Functions. Let $F(s)$ be the distribution function of a probability measure $P$ on $\bbr$. Then:

  1. We have \begin{equation}\notag \lim_{s\to \infty} F(s) = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \lim_{s\to -\infty} F(s) = 0. \end{equation}

  2. $F(s)$ is a non-decreasing function, in the sense that \begin{equation}\notag s\leq t \quad \Rightarrow \quad F(s) \leq F(t). \end{equation}

  3. $F(s)$ is right-continuous at every $s\in \bbr$, in the sense that \begin{equation}\notag F(s) = \lim_{t\to s^+} F(t). \end{equation}

  4. The probability measure $P$ is discrete if and only if $F(s)$ is a step function.

  5. The probability measure $P$ is continuous if and only if $F(s)$ is continuous.

Though we won’t prove them, I am hoping that the equivalences in (4) and (5) at least seem plausible in light of the examples above. We will actually address one half of the equivalence of (5) again when we state the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (probability version).

Technically, the form of ‘continuity’ in part (5) that the distribution function $F(s)$ must have is stronger than the usual form we encounter in elementary calculus. If you care to learn more, read:

Looking beyond… This stronger form of continuity is called absolute continuity. In addition to regular continuity of $F(s)$, this form of continuity requires that the derivative $F’(s)$ exists almost everywhere. This fact will be important below when we relate distribution functions to density functions.

Now:

Problem Prompt. Time for some practice with distribution functions. Do problems 15 and 16 on the worksheet.

It turns out that there is a very tight connection between distribution functions of continuous probability measures on $\bbr$ and density functions. This connection is expressed in:

The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (probability version). Let $F(s)$ be the distribution function of a probability measure $P$ on $\bbr$. If $F(s)$ is continuous, then:

  1. The measure $P$ is continuous.

  2. Wherever the derivative $F’(s)$ exists, we have \begin{equation}\notag F’(s) = f(s), \end{equation} where $f(s)$ is the density function of $P$.

To see why this is not just the standard Fundamental Theorem of Calculus that we learned in elementary calculus, recall that the latter begins with an integrable $f(s)$ and produces the antiderivative $F(s)$ via the formula

\begin{equation}\notag F(s) = \int_{-\infty}^s f(t) \ \d t. \end{equation}

This version of the Fundamental Theorem, however, goes in the opposite direction: It begins with a continuous distribution function $F(s)$ and produces the density function $f(s)$ as the derivative of $F(s)$.

Again, the form of ‘continuity’ of $F(s)$ referred to in this theorem is actually the stronger form called absolute continuity that I mentioned in the “Looking beyond…” box above, where I also mentioned that the places where the derivative $F’(s)$ exists happens to be almost everywhere. You will how this works in practice in a simple example in the Problem Prompt below—but first:

Looking beyond… This “probability version” of the Fundamental Theorem is actually properly known as the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus for Lebesgue Integrals.

Now, let’s have a go at applying the Fundamental Theorem:

Problem Prompt. Do problem 17 on the worksheet.

Having studied distribution functions, we now study their (almost) inverses:

Definition. Let $P$ be a probability measure on $\bbr$ with distribution function $F:\bbr \to [0,1]$. The quantile function $Q: [0,1] \to \bbr$ is defined so that

\begin{equation}\notag Q(p) = \min\{s\in \bbr : F(s)\geq p\}. \end{equation}

In other words, the value $s=Q(p)$ is the smallest $s\in \bbr$ such that $F(s) \geq p$.

  1. The value $Q(p)$ is denoted $\phi_p$ by your textbook and is called the $p$-th quantile.

  2. The quantile $\phi_{0.5}$ is called the median of the probability measure $P$.

It is helpful to remember that a distribution function $F:\bbr \to [0,1]$ takes real numbers $s\in \bbr$ as inputs, and returns probabilities $p\in[0,1]$ as outputs. On the other hand, a quantile function $Q:[0,1]\to \bbr$ goes in the opposite direction: It takes probabilities as $p\in[0,1]$ as inputs, and returns real numbers $s\in \bbr$ as outputs. Thus:

\begin{equation}\label{inv-eqn} Q(p) = s \quad \text{and} \quad p = F(s). \end{equation}

Be careful in interpreting these two equations simultaneously, however, since $F(s)$ might not be an invertible function! (As an extreme example, $F(s)$ could be a step function, as we saw above.)

To illustrate, let’s return to the continuous probability measure $P$ from above with density

\begin{equation}\notag f(s) = \begin{cases} \displaystyle \frac{1}{s^2} & : s\geq 1, \\ 0 & : \text{otherwise}.\end{cases} \end{equation}

The graph of $f(s)$ is:

Remember that probabilities are interpreted as areas under the graph of $f(s)$.

The number $s = Q(0.5)$ is the location $s\in \bbr$ along the horizontal axis where half of the probability is to the left:

From the figure, we see that $Q(0.5)=2$, or $\phi_{0.5}=2$ in the book’s notation.

Likewise, the number $s = Q(0.75)$ is the location $s\in \bbr$ along the horizontal axis where $75\%$ of the probability is to the left:

From this figure, we see that $Q(0.75) = 4$, or $\phi_{0.75}=4$ in the book’s notation.

We may confirm these numbers by using the formula for the distribution function $F(s)$ that we computed above:

\begin{equation}\notag F(s) = \begin{cases} -\displaystyle\frac{1}{s} + 1 & : s\geq 1, \\ 0 & : s <1 .\end{cases} \end{equation}

To compute $s = Q(0.5)$, we solve:

\begin{equation}\notag F(s) = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow\quad -\frac{1}{s} + 1 = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s=2. \end{equation}

Thus, $Q(0.5)=2$, just like we saw in the figure.

Likewise, to compute $s = Q(0.75)$, we solve:

\begin{equation}\notag F(s) = 0.75 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\frac{1}{s} + 1 = 0.75 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s=4. \end{equation}

Thus, $Q(0.75)=4$.

Computing quantiles in the continuous case is facilitated by visualizing probabilities as areas under density curves. In the discrete case, however, things can be slightly trickier. You will get practice in:

Problem Prompt. Do problems 18 and 19 on the worksheet.

11. Bivariate continuous probability measures

Having studied continuous probability measures on the real line $\bbr$, it is now not too much of a leap to imagine the definition of a continuous probability measure on the plane $\bbr^2$. In this scenario, the interval $[a,b]$ on the real line $\bbr$ is replaced with the rectangle

\begin{equation}\notag [a,b] \times [c,d] \defeq \{(s,t) \in \bbr^2 : a\leq s \leq b, \ c\leq t \leq d\} \end{equation}

in the plane $\bbr^2$:

As a matter of terminology, continuous probability measures on $\bbr$ are called univariate or single-variable, while continuous probability measures on $\bbr^2$ are called bivariate or two-variable.

Here’s the definition:

Definition. Let $P$ be a probability measure on $\bbr^2$. We shall say $P$ is continuous if there is a function $f:\bbr^2 \to \bbr$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag P\big( [a,b] \times [c,d] \big) = \int_c^d \int_a^b f(s,t) \ \d s \d t \end{equation}

for all

\begin{equation}\notag -\infty \leq a \leq b \leq +\infty \quad \text{and} \quad -\infty \leq c \leq d \leq +\infty. \end{equation}

In this case, the function $f$ is called the probability density function (PDF) of the probability measure $P$, and $\bbr^2$ is called a continuous probability space (when equipped with $P$).

Thus, the major difference between univariate and bivariate continuous probability measures are the number of variables and the number of integrals (one versus two). Essentially all the remarks (or their obvious bivariate analogs) that I made after the definition of univariate continuous probability measures apply also to bivariate continuous probability measures.

Problem Prompt. Do problems 20 and 21 on the worksheet.

We have the following list of properties of bivariate density functions. This is now the third version of this same list of properties!

Properties of Probability Density Functions (bivariate version). Let $f(s,t)$ be the probability density function of a continuous probability measure $P$ on $\bbr^2$. Then:

  1. $f(s,t)\geq 0 $ for all $s,t\in \bbr$, and

  2. $\int_{-\infty}^\infty \int_{-\infty}^\infty f(s,t) \ \d s \d t =1$.

And we also have a third version of the Probability Construction Lemma. (Are you detecting the pattern yet…?)

Continuous Probability Construction Lemma (bivariate version). Let $f:\bbr^2 \to \bbr$ be a function such that

  1. $f(s,t)\geq 0$ for all $s,t\in \bbr$, and

  2. $\int_{-\infty}^\infty \int_{-\infty}^\infty f(s,t) \ \d s \d t =1$.

Then there is a unique continuous probability measure $P$ on $\bbr^2$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag P\big( [a,b] \times [c,d] \big) = \int_c^d \int_a^b f(s,t) \ \d s \d t \end{equation}

for all

\begin{equation}\notag -\infty \leq a \leq b \leq +\infty \quad \text{and} \quad -\infty \leq c \leq d \leq +\infty. \end{equation}

Then:

Problem Prompt. Do problem 22 on the worksheet.

Having seen the definitions of univariate and bivariate continuous probability measures, I bet you will have no trouble imagining the definition of a general multivariate continuous probability measure. For example, in the three-variable case, all integrals become triple integrals, and so forth.

12. Probability density graphs (bivariate versions)

We may visualize bivariate density functions by first imagining the sample space $S=\bbr^2$ as the horizontal coordinate plane in $3$-dimensional space $\bbr^3$. Then, we may draw the graph of a density function $f(s,t)$ in $\bbr^3$, just like we learned in (for example) multivariable calculus. But remember, since $f(s,t)$ is a two-variable real-valued function, its graph is not a curve, but rather a surface. As in the univariate case, these graphs are called probability density graphs.

Here’s a bivariate density function from the worksheet:

\begin{equation}\notag f(s,t) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{8}(s+t) & : 0 \leq s, t\leq 2, \\ 0 & : \text{otherwise}. \end{cases} \end{equation}

Over the square $[0,2] \times [0,2]$ in the $st$-plane, I am hoping that you recognize the graph of $f(s,t)$ is a plane through the origin, with a slope of $1/8$ in both the $s$- and $t$-directions. For points $(s,t)$ that are not in this square, we have $f(s,t) = 0$. Here’s the picture:

In this picture, it appears that the graph of $f(s,t)$ doesn’t exist for $(s,t)$ outside the square mentioned above, but that’s only because I didn’t want to clutter the picture. So, you have to imagine that the graph of $f(s,t)$ drops to the $st$-plane for points $(s,t)$ outside the square $[0,2]\times [0,2]$.

Remember in the univariate case that probabilities are understood visually as areas under density curves. Here, in the bivariate case, probabilities are volumes under density surfaces. So, for example, the probability

\begin{equation}\notag P \big( [0,1] \times [0,1] \big) = \int_0^1 \int_0^1 f(s,t) \ \d s \d t = \frac{1}{8} \end{equation}

is the volume under the plane and above the square $[0,1]\times [0,1]$ in the $st$-plane. And again, I must issue the following reminder:

The heights of the density surface are not probabilities.

For another example, consider the density

\begin{equation}\notag f(s,t) = \begin{cases} e^{-(s+t)} & : s,t>0, \\ 0 & : \text{otherwise}, \end{cases} \end{equation}

which is also from the worksheet. The density graph is:

As with any continuous probability measure on $\bbr^2$, we showed that $P(\bbr^2)=1$. This means that the total volume underneath the graph of this surface is equal to $1$.
